title_en: Scientists Thought Parkinson’s Was in Our Genes. It Might Be in the Water
title_zh: 科学家们曾以为帕金森病在我们基因里，但它可能与饮水有关
rubric_en: New ideas about chronic illness could revolutionize treatment, if we take the research seriously.
rubric_zh: 慢性病的新思路可能彻底改变治疗方式，前提是我们认真对待这些研究。

paragraphs.en: Amy Lindberg spent 26 years in the Navy and she still walked like it—with intention, like her chin had someplace to be. But around 2017, her right foot stopped following orders. Lindberg and her husband Brad were five years into their retirement. After moving 10 times for Uncle Sam, they’d bought their dream house near the North Carolina coast. They had a backyard that spilled out onto wetlands. From the kitchen, you could see cranes hunting. They kept bees and played pickleball and watched their children grow.
paragraphs.zh: 艾米·林德伯格在海军服役了26年，她走路时仍像军人一样——动作有分寸、目标明确，下巴都像是有地方要去似的。大约2017年前后，她的右脚开始“不听指挥”。林德伯格和丈夫布拉德退休后已经五年。随军迁徙了10次后，他们在北卡罗来纳州海岸线附近买下了梦寐以求的房子。那儿有一座通向湿地的后院，从厨房望去，还能看到鹈鹕在猎食。他们养蜜蜂、打匹克球，见证孩子们长大。

paragraphs.en: But now Lindberg’s right foot was out of rhythm. She worked hard to ignore it, but she couldn’t disregard the tremors. And she’d started to misplace words and thoughts, especially when she got excited. Was this normal? She was 57, fit and clean-living. Could the culprit be menopause?
paragraphs.zh: 但现在林德伯格的右脚完全走乱了节奏。她努力把它放在心里不去理会，但颤抖她再也忽视不了。她还开始在措辞和思绪上出现卡顿，尤其是兴奋的时候更明显。这是正常的吗？她57岁，身体健康，生活方式也很规律。是不是更年期在作怪？

paragraphs.en: The diagnosis took all of five minutes. Lindberg had Parkinson’s disease, the neurologist said, with all the classic symptoms. PD—as the scientists she would meet call it—is a neurological disorder, and a life sentence. Sufferers gradually lose control of their muscles, their bowels, their esophagus. Doctors told Lindberg that there was no way to know what had caused it.
paragraphs.zh: 医生在五分钟内就给出了诊断。神经科医生说林德伯格得了帕金森病，表现出了所有典型症状。PD——也就是她后来会见到的那些科学家所说的——是一种神经系统疾病，也是“终身判决”。患者会逐步失去对肌肉、肠道以及食管的控制。医生告诉林德伯格，没有办法知道是什么原因造成的。

paragraphs.en: Lindberg spent years in the military, around Camp Lejeune.
paragraphs.zh: 林德伯格在军队里服役了很多年，她曾长期待在利乔恩营附近。

paragraphs.en: The daughter of a sailor, Lindberg had built her life around the military. She was commissioned in the Navy out of college and became an officer at 23. Her first posting was to Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune in North Carolina, a city-sized training hub that supports more than 60,000 sailors and marines. There were murmurs even then—whispers of weird cancers and stillbirths—but Lejeune was one of the prettier pieces of land in the Navy’s property portfolio. The bachelor officers’ quarters were on a grassy thumb of shoreline called Paradise Point, where the New River meets the Atlantic.
paragraphs.zh: 林德伯格出生于一个水手家庭，后来把生活几乎全部建立在军队之中。她大学毕业后获委任进入海军，23 岁时成为军官。她的第一站是位于北卡罗来纳州的马里纳部队训练基地莱琼营（Camp Lejeune），这是一个可容纳 6 万多名海军和海军陆战队员的准城市级训练中心。即便当时也有人低声议论——关于奇怪的癌症病例和死胎的传闻——但莱琼营在海军地产里一直被视作风景最优美的地段之一。海军军官单身宿舍坐落在一块草地般的海岸“指状”地带“天堂点”（Paradise Point），新河在这里与大西洋交汇。

paragraphs.en: “Lejeune was just picturesque,” Lindberg says. “We had a river right there, and the beach wasn’t far away, and you worked half a mile from where you lived.” She loved her job at the hospital and made lifelong friends. She met her husband—a photo on her desk shows a blond Lindberg beaming beneath her Navy cap while Brad smiles broadly in his dress blues. “It was really nice,” she says. “You’d never suspect the water.”
paragraphs.zh: “莱琼营确实很美，”林德伯格说，“那里就有一条河，海滩也不远，工作地点离住处只有半英里。”她热爱在医院的工作，并结交了终身好友。她与丈夫就是在这里相识——她桌上的一张照片里，一个戴着海军帽、笑容灿烂的金发林德伯格站在前，布拉德穿着礼服蓝军装，笑得很开怀。她说：“那段日子真是非常美好。你绝对不会怀疑那里的水。”

paragraphs.en: Parkinson’s is the second most common neurological disease in the United States, after Alzheimer’s; each year 90,000 Americans are diagnosed. For decades, Parkinson’s research has focused on genetics, on finding the rogue letters in our genome that cause this incurable misery. Today, published research on the genetics behind Parkinson’s outnumbers all other potential causes six to one. This is partially because one of the disease’s most generous benefactors, Google cofounder Sergey Brin, can tie Parkinson’s to his genetics. Some Parkinson’s patients diagnosed before age 50—as Michael J. Fox was—can trace the disease to their genes; Brin, whose mother has the disease, carries a mutation of the LRRK2 gene, which significantly increases the likelihood of him developing PD. Over the years, Fox’s foundation has raised billions for Parkinson’s research, and Brin has personally committed $1.8 billion to fighting the disorder. All told, more than half of Parkinson’s research dollars in the past two decades have flowed toward genetics.
paragraphs.zh: 在美国，帕金森病是仅次于阿尔茨海默病的第二大神经系统疾病；每年有 9 万名美国人被确诊。几十年来，帕金森病研究一直聚焦在遗传学上，寻找基因组里那些导致这种绝症般痛苦的“坏因子”。如今，关于帕金森病遗传学的已发表研究，其数量是所有其他可能成因研究的六倍。这在某种程度上源于该病最慷慨的资助者之一——谷歌联合创始人谢尔盖·布林——能够把自己的帕金森病与遗传背景联系起来。部分在 50 岁前被诊断出帕金森病的患者——比如迈克尔·J·福克斯——可以追溯到家系遗传；布林的母亲患有该病，他本人携带一种 LRRK2 基因突变，这会显著提高其患 PD（帕金森病）的可能性。多年来，福克斯基金会已为帕金森病研究筹集了数十亿美元，而布林本人也已个人承诺投入 18 亿美元与该疾病作斗争。综合来看，过去二十年里，超过一半的帕金森病研究资金都流向了遗传学方向。

paragraphs.en: But Parkinson’s rates in the US have doubled in the past 30 years. And studies suggest they will climb another 15 to 35 percent in each coming decade. This is not how an inherited genetic disease is supposed to behave.
paragraphs.zh: 但在过去 30 年里，美国帕金森病发病率已经翻倍。研究显示，它在未来每个十年还可能再上升 15% 到 35%。这并不符合一种遗传性疾病应有的表现方式。

paragraphs.en: Despite the avalanche of funding, the latest research suggests that only 10 to 15 percent of Parkinson’s cases can be fully explained by genetics. The other three-quarters are, functionally, a mystery. “More than two-thirds of people with PD don’t have any clear genetic link,” says Briana De Miranda, a researcher at the University of Alabama at Birmingham. “So, we’re moving to a new question: What else could it be?”
paragraphs.zh: 尽管资金大量涌入，最新研究表明，只有10%到15%的帕金森病（PD）病例可以完全用遗传因素解释。其余四分之三在功能上仍然是个谜。阿拉巴马大学伯明翰分校的研究员Briana De Miranda说：“超过三分之二的PD患者没有任何明确的遗传关联。” “所以，我们正在转向一个新问题：还可能是什么原因？”

paragraphs.en: Briana De Miranda, a researcher at the University of Alabama at Birmingham.
paragraphs.zh: 布丽安娜·德·米兰达，阿拉巴马大学伯明翰分校研究员。

paragraphs.en: “The health you enjoy or don’t enjoy today is a function of your environment in the past,” says Ray Dorsey, a physician and professor of neurology at the University of Rochester. Your “environment” could be the refinery a town over, the lead in the paint of your mother’s home, the plastic sheath of the Hot Pocket you microwaved in 1996. It is air pollution and PFAS and pesticides and so much more.
paragraphs.zh: 罗彻斯特大学神经科教授、医生Ray Dorsey说：“你今天享有或未能享有的健康，是你过去环境的结果。”你的“环境”可能是城镇对面的炼油厂、你母亲家里墙纸中的铅、你在1996年微波加热Hot Pocket时用到的塑料外壳。它还包括空气污染、PFAS、农药，以及更多其他因素。

paragraphs.en: And this environment of yours—the sum of all your exposures, from conception to the grave—could be making you sicker than you realize. In a study of half a million Britons, Oxford researchers determined that lifestyle and the environment is 10 times more likely to explain early death than genetics. But that also offers a tantalizing prospect. If Parkinson’s is an environmental disease, as Dorsey and a small band of researchers emphatically believe, then maybe we can end it.
paragraphs.zh: 而且这个环境——从受孕到去世的全部暴露总和——可能让你比自己意识到的更容易生病。在一项涉及五十万英国人的研究中，牛津大学研究人员发现，生活方式和环境因素导致早逝的可能性比遗传因素高10倍。不过这也带来了一个令人振奋的前景。如果像Dorsey和一小群研究者坚定认为的那样，帕金森病是环境性疾病，那么也许我们有机会让它终结。

paragraphs.en: In 1982, two years before Lindberg was stationed at Camp Lejeune, a 42-year-old heroin addict named George Carillo was wheeled into the Santa Clara Valley Medical Center in San Jose, California. A few days earlier, Carillo had been perfectly able-bodied. Now he was mute and unable to move. Baffled, the neurologists on call came to an impossible diagnosis: The patient, over a long weekend, had developed Parkinson’s disease.
paragraphs.zh: 1982年，也就是林德堡调到利文琼营区（Camp Lejeune）前两年，一名42岁的海洛因成瘾者乔治·卡里洛（George Carillo）被推入加州圣何塞圣克拉拉谷医疗中心（Santa Clara Valley Medical Center）。几天前，卡里洛还十分健壮。如今，他却说不出话，无法行动。值班的神经科医生们一头雾水，得出了一个几乎不可思议的诊断：患者在一个周末长假里，竟然罹患了帕金森病。

paragraphs.en: Carillo would probably have spent the rest of his short life in a psych ward had a pioneering young neurologist named Bill Langston not intervened. The way Parkinson’s takes over the body is distinct, Langston told me. The disease attacks the neurons in a region of the brain called the substantia nigra, a small dark structure that stands out amid the squirms of beige. The neurons here release dopamine, which sends signals to other neurons that help the body to move smoothly and effectively. In Parkinson’s these neurons die off; by the time a patient is diagnosed, they have often lost 60 to 80 percent of them. The process usually takes years, Langston says. But in the case of Carillo, all the neurons had disappeared almost overnight.
paragraphs.zh: 若不是一位名叫比尔·兰斯顿（Bill Langston）的年轻先锋神经科医生出手干预，卡里洛很可能会在短暂的人生后半段余下时间里一直住在精神科病房。兰斯顿告诉我，帕金森病夺走身体控制权的方式非常独特。该病会攻击脑内一个叫做黑质（substantia nigra）的区域中的神经元，这是一个在米色组织间格外突出的黑色小结构。这个区域的神经元会释放多巴胺，向其他神经元发出信号，使身体动作平顺而有效。帕金森病时这些神经元会逐渐死亡；到患者被诊断时，他们通常已经丢失了60%到80%。这一过程通常持续多年，兰斯顿说。但在卡里洛的病例中，几乎所有神经元都在一夜之间消失了。

paragraphs.en: Over the summer of 1982, Langston found five more “frozen addicts” across the Bay Area. Through gumshoe detective work, he discovered they had all injected a batch of what they believed to be a designer drug called MPPP, cooked in a Morgan Hill basement. But the chemistry had gone awry. Instead of 1-methyl-4-phenyl-4-propionoxypiperidine, a potent opioid with morphine-like effects, the dime-bag chemist had accidentally made 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine, or MPTP, a pharmacological slipup that would rewrite neurology textbooks.
paragraphs.zh: 1982年夏天，兰斯顿在旧金山湾区又找到了五名“冻结症”吸毒者。通过近乎侦探式的调查，他发现他们都注射了一批他们以为是名为MPPP的“设计毒品”，是在摩根山（Morgan Hill）一个地下室里煮制的。但化学反应出了偏差。那位“买来一角酒吧硬币装药剂”的制毒者本该合成的是1-甲基-4-苯基-4-丙酮氧基哌啶（1-methyl-4-phenyl-4-propionoxypiperidine），这是一种具有吗啡样效应的强效阿片类药物，却意外地做出了1-甲基-4-苯基-1,2,3,6-四氢吡啶，即MPTP。正是这场药理学上的失误，改变了神经病学教材。

paragraphs.en: When Langston and colleagues secured a batch of MPTP and tested it on primates, they knew they had uncorked a revolution. “Any neurologist could see these monkeys and immediately know that’s Parkinson’s,” Langston says—which was especially compelling, since monkeys do not get Parkinson’s in the wild. In a first, Langston showed that MPTP killed the dopamine-producing neurons in monkeys’ substantia nigra. The discovery made him the most famous Parkinson’s researcher in the country and, Langston wrote at the time, promised to “turn the entire field of Parkinson’s disease upside down.” Parkinson’s, it appeared, could be caused by a chemical.
paragraphs.zh: 当兰斯顿和同事拿到一批MPTP并在灵长类动物身上进行测试后，他们意识到自己正在掀起一场革命。兰斯顿说：“任何神经科医生一看到这些猴子，都能立刻判断那就是帕金森病。”这一点尤其令人信服，因为野生猴子并不会得帕金森病。首次研究中，兰斯顿证实了MPTP会杀死猴子黑质中的多巴胺生成神经元。这个发现使他成为全国最著名的帕金森病研究者之一，也让兰斯顿当时写道，该发现有望“把帕金森病这个整个学科彻底颠覆”。看起来，帕金森病确实可能是由一种化学物质引起的。

paragraphs.en: Amy Lindberg settled quickly into life at Lejeune. She played tennis and ran on her lunch breaks, flitting through sprinklers in the turgid Carolina summers. But something dark was lurking beneath her feet.
paragraphs.zh: 艾米·林德堡很快就适应了在勒让努的生活。她会打网球、在午休时跑步，夏天闷热时会穿梭在喷头里的水雾中。但有一股黑暗正在她脚下潜伏。

paragraphs.en: Sometime before 1953, a massive plume of trichlorethylene, or TCE, had entered the groundwater beneath Camp Lejeune. TCE is a highly effective solvent—one of those midcentury wonder chemicals—that vaporizes quickly and dissolves whatever grease it touches. The spill’s source is debated, but grunts on base used TCE to maintain machinery, and the dry cleaner sprayed it on dress blues. It was ubiquitous at Lejeune and all over America.
paragraphs.zh: 在 1953 年之前的某个时候，大量三氯乙烯（TCE）污染羽流进入了勒让努基地下方的地下水。TCE 是一种高效溶剂——那种上世纪中叶的“奇迹化学品”——会快速挥发，并溶解接触到的任何油脂。污染来源一直有争议，但基地里的军士兵用 TCE 维护机械，干洗店则把它喷在军装制服上。它在勒让努以及美国各地都很常见。

paragraphs.en: And TCE appeared benign, too—you could rub it on your hands or huff its fumes and feel no immediate effects. It plays a longer game. For approximately 35 years, Marines and sailors who lived at Lejeune unknowingly breathed in vaporized TCE whenever they turned on their tap. The Navy, which oversees the Marine Corps, first denied the toxic plume’s existence, then refused to admit it could affect Marines’ health. But as Lejeune’s vets aged, cancers and unexplained illness began stalking them at staggering rates. Marines stationed on base had a 35 percent higher risk of developing kidney cancer, a 47 percent higher risk of Hodgkin’s lymphoma, a 68 percent higher risk of multiple myeloma. At the local cemetery, the section reserved for infants had to be expanded.
paragraphs.zh: 而且 TCE 似乎也无害——你可以把它擦在手上或闻一闻它的蒸气，短时间内也不会有明显感觉。它却在“拖延”生效。大约三十五年里，驻守勒让努的海军陆战队员和水兵在不知不觉中每次开水龙头时都吸入了挥发后的 TCE。海军（海军陆战队隶属于海军）起初否认这种毒性羽流的存在，随后又拒绝承认它会影响海军陆战队员的健康。然而，随着勒让努退役军人逐渐老去，癌症和莫名其妙的疾病以惊人的速度追上了他们。驻地部队中的海军陆战队员其肾癌风险高出 35%，霍奇金淋巴瘤风险高出 47%，多发性骨髓瘤风险高出 68%。当地公墓里，专门为婴儿预留的区域也不得不扩大。

paragraphs.en: Meanwhile, Langston had spent the remainder of the 1980s setting up the California Parkinson’s Foundation (later renamed the Parkinson’s Institute), a lab and treatment facility equipped with everything needed to finally reveal the cause of the disease. “We thought we were going to solve it,” Langston told me. Researchers affiliated with the institute created the first animal model for Parkinson’s, identified a pesticide called Paraquat as a near chemical match to MPTP, and proved that farm workers who sprayed Paraquat developed Parkinson’s at exceedingly high rates. Then they showed that identical twins developed Parkinson’s at the same rate as fraternal twins—something that wouldn’t make sense if the disease were purely genetic, since identical twins share DNA and fraternal twins do not. They even noted TCE as a potential cause of the disease, Langston says. Each revelation, the team thought, represented another nail in the coffin of the genetic theory of Parkinson’s.
paragraphs.zh: 与此同时，兰克斯顿在八十年代后半段创立了加州帕金森基金会（后改名为帕金森研究所），并配备了一个实验室和治疗设施，具备了最终揭开这种疾病病因所需的一切条件。“我们以为自己就要找出答案了，”兰克斯顿告诉我。与该研究所有关的研究人员建立了第一个帕金森病动物模型，找出了名为百草枯的农药，它在化学特性上几乎和 MPTP 完全吻合，并证明喷洒百草枯的农场工人罹患帕金森病的比例高得离谱。随后他们还发现，异卵双胞胎与同卵双胞胎的发病率相同；如果这种疾病纯粹由遗传决定，这一结果是解释不通的，因为同卵双胞胎共享 DNA，而异卵双胞胎并不共享。兰克斯顿说，他们甚至将 TCE 也列为可能的病因。每一项发现，团队都认为，都是给帕金森病“遗传说”这口棺材钉上的另一颗钉子。

paragraphs.en: But there was a problem. The Human Genome Project had launched in 1990, promising to usher in a new era of personalized medicine. The project’s goal, to identify all of the genes in man, was radical, and by the time it was completed in 2000, frothy comparisons to the moon landing were frequent. Unraveling our genome would “revolutionize the diagnosis, prevention, and treatment of most, if not all, human diseases,” then president Bill Clinton said.
paragraphs.zh: 然而事情并不那么简单。人类基因组计划于1990年启动，承诺开启个性化医学的新时代。该计划旨在识别男性体内全部基因，这一目标在当时被视为激进之举；到2000年完成时，人们频繁拿它与登月行动作比较。克林顿总统当时说，解读人类基因组将“革命性地改变几乎所有乃至全部人类疾病的诊断、预防和治疗”。

paragraphs.en: But for Langston and his colleagues, the Human Genome Project sucked the air out of the environmental health space. Genetics became the “800-pound gorilla,” as one scientist put it. “All the research dollars went toward genetics,” says Sam Goldman, who worked with Langston on the twin study. “It’s just a lot sexier than epidemiology. It’s the latest gadget, the bigger rocket.” A generation of young scientists were being trained to think of genetics and genomics as the default place to look for answers. “I characterize science as a bunch of 5-year-olds playing soccer,” says another researcher. “They all go where the ball is, running around the field in a herd.” And the ball was decidedly not environmental health. “Donors want a cure,” Langston says. “And they want it now.”
paragraphs.zh: 但对Langston及其同事而言，人类基因组计划几乎抽走了环境健康领域的活力。正如一位科学家所说，遗传学成了“举足轻重的‘八百磅大猩猩’”。“所有研究经费都流向了遗传学。”与Langston一起做这对双胞胎研究的Sam Goldman说，“它比流行病学更‘性感’。它是最新的高科技产品，是更大的火箭。”一代年轻科学家被培养成默认先去遗传学和基因组学中寻找答案。另一位研究者说：“我把科学形容为一群五岁孩子踢足球——他们都去追着球，成群地在场上跑。”而这颗球显然不在环境健康上。“捐助者想要治愈方法，”Langston说，“而且要立刻要。”

paragraphs.en: In 1997, researchers found a family in Italy that had passed along Parkinson’s disease for generations. Although the gene in question would later be shown to cause just a fraction of Parkinson’s cases, the damage was done. The Parkinson’s Institute faced stronger economic headwinds and difficulties with administration, and Langston eventually chose to shut it down. The environmental theory of Parkinson’s went back on the shelf.
paragraphs.zh: 1997年，研究人员在意大利发现了一个家族，帕金森病在这个家系中世代遗传。尽管后来证明该基因仅与一小部分帕金森病例有关，这一认识仍然产生了严重影响。帕金森研究所面临更大的经济压力和行政管理困难，Langston最终决定关闭它。帕金森病的环境学说被重新搁置起来。

paragraphs.en: No one knows exactly how much of the world’s drinking water is laced with TCE. The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention reckons that the water supply of between 4 and 18 percent of Americans is contaminated, although not always at dangerous concentrations; the Environmental Working Group figures 17 million Americans drink the stuff. In Silicon Valley, where TCE was integral to the manufacturing of early transistors, a necklace of underground plumes have been identified along Highway 101 from Palo Alto to San Jose. Santa Clara County has more toxic Superfund sites, at 23, than any other county in the country. (Several tech giants have offices near or on top of these sites; in 2013, workers at a Google office were subjected to unhealthy levels of TCE for months after a ventilation system failed.)
paragraphs.zh: 无人确切知道世界饮用水中有多少含有三氯乙烯（TCE）。美国疾病控制与预防中心估计，美国4%到18%的人口饮用水受污染，虽然不一定都达到危险浓度；环境工作组则表示有1700万美国人正在喝这种物质。在硅谷，三氯乙烯曾是早期晶体管制造的关键成分，在从帕洛阿尔托到圣何塞的101号公路沿线，已经发现了一串地下污染羽流。圣克拉拉县拥有全国最多的有毒超级基金场地，达到23个，超过任何其他县。（一些科技巨头的办公地点就在这些场地附近或其上方；2013年，谷歌一个办公室的员工在通风系统故障后的数月内，持续暴露在不健康水平的三氯乙烯中。）

paragraphs.en: And while TCE’s connection to cancer is well studied, what it does to our brain is more mysterious. That’s because good data on exposure is devilishly hard to come by. The US, with its fractious health care system, has few national databases, and chemical exposures are rarely tracked.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管TCE与癌症之间的联系已被广泛研究，但它对大脑的影响仍然更神秘。这是因为关于暴露程度的可靠数据极其难以获得。美国的医疗体系本身争议不断，拥有的全国性数据库很少，而化学物质暴露也几乎不被系统记录。

paragraphs.en: Sam Goldman at his home in San Francisco. His research compared Camp Pendleton in California with Lejeune.
paragraphs.zh: 在旧金山的家中，Sam Goldman。 他的研究将加州的坎普·潘德尔顿海军陆战队基地与莱琼营进行了比较。

paragraphs.en: In 2017, Sam Goldman realized that Camp Lejeune offered the perfect opportunity to change this. Goldman—an epidemiologist and a doctor—has made a career out of teasing apart data: finding unusual case reports, looking for patterns, interviewing patients in the clinic about what chemicals they handled at old jobs and what exposures they faced in their childhood. In the case of Lejeune, Goldman could examine VA medical records to find Parkinson’s diagnoses and compare them to service records. But Goldman’s genius wasn’t finding this Lejeune cohort—it was realizing he had a control group, too.
paragraphs.zh: 2017年，Sam Goldman意识到，莱琼营为改变这一局面提供了绝佳机会。Goldman 是一名流行病学家兼医生，他一生致力于梳理复杂数据：寻找异常病例报告、寻找规律、在诊室里采访患者，了解他们年轻时在工作中接触过哪些化学品，以及童年时期遭遇过哪些暴露。以莱琼营为例，Goldman 可以查阅退伍军人事务部的医疗记录，找到帕金森病诊断病例，并将其与服役记录进行对比。但Goldman 的天才之处并不在于找到莱琼营这一人群，而在于他意识到自己同时拥有了一个对照组。

paragraphs.en: Camp Pendleton, in Southern California, is the Marine Corps’ West Coast equivalent to Lejeune. Thousands of young, healthy Marines shuffle through its barbed-wired gates each year. But Pendleton has one thing Lejeune does not: uncontaminated drinking water.
paragraphs.zh: 位于南加州的坎普·潘德尔顿是海军陆战队在西海岸对应莱琼营的基地。每年都有成千上万名年轻健康的海军陆战队员穿过其带刺铁丝的围栏。然而，潘德尔顿拥有莱琼营所没有的一点：未受污染的饮用水。

paragraphs.en: When Goldman compared both populations, the results were shocking: Marines exposed to TCE at Lejeune were 70 percent more likely to have Parkinson’s than those stationed at Pendleton. And in a follow-up study last year, he showed that disease progression in Lejeune vets with the highest exposure to TCE was faster than those with low or no exposure, too. In the world of Parkinson’s research, Goldman’s study was a blockbuster.
paragraphs.zh: 当戈德曼比较这两个群体时，结果令人震惊：在利昂（Lejeune）基地服役、接触三氯乙烯（TCE）的海军陆战队员，患帕金森病的风险比驻扎在彭德尔顿（Pendleton）的高出70%。而在去年的一项追踪研究中，他还发现，在利昂接受最高剂量TCE暴露的退伍军人中，疾病进展也比低暴露或未暴露者更快。在帕金森病研究领域，戈德曼的这项研究可谓轰动一时。

paragraphs.en: But to really prove a link, you need more than just correlation. So, on the third floor of a drab university building in Birmingham, Alabama, Briana De Miranda has re-created Camp Lejeune in her lab, but for mice.
paragraphs.zh: 但要真正证明二者存在关联，单凭相关性是不够的。因此，在阿拉巴马州伯明翰一幢灰暗大学建筑的三楼，布里亚娜·德·米兰达（Briana De Miranda）在实验室里重建了利昂营地——只是对象是小鼠。

paragraphs.en: De Miranda is a toxicologist, not a neurologist, which is an unusual CV for a cutting-edge Parkinson’s researcher. When I visit her in October 2024, she shows me the plexiglass chamber where a few dozen mice doze in a pile. They’ve been spending their days in this chamber for months, inhaling a small amount of TCE almost every day. This experiment is the first to re-create the exposure someone like Lindberg experienced over years at Camp Lejeune.
paragraphs.zh: 德·米兰达是一名毒理学家，而非神经科医生，这在前沿帕金森病研究者中并不常见。2024年10月我访问她时，她带我看了一个透明的亚克力舱，里面有几十只小鼠成堆打盹。几个月来，它们几乎每天都在这个舱内吸入少量TCE。这个实验是第一次在实验条件下重现像林德伯格（Lindberg）那样的人在利昂营地多年遭受的暴露过程。

paragraphs.en: De Miranda walks into a dark annex of her lab and asks a tech to pull up some imagery. “These are dopamine neurons in the brain,” De Miranda says, pointing to a scan of the control mice. In unexposed mice the substantia nigra looks like a nighttime satellite image of Manhattan—thousands of neurons sending dopamine across the mice’s brains to orchestrate fluid scurrying and sniffing and munching. Then the tech pulls up the brain scans of mice who have been exposed to TCE. Suddenly we’re in West Virginia. It’s not pitch black, but most of the lights are off and the ones that remain have been dimmed. The dopamine neurons have died, De Miranda explains. And she’s seeing the physical effects in the mice too. “We see minor motion defects; we see it in their gait, and we are seeing cognitive effects,” De Miranda says.
paragraphs.zh: 德·米兰达走进实验室的一间昏暗附属室，请技术人员调出影像。“这是大脑中的多巴胺神经元，”德·米兰达指着对照组小鼠的扫描图说。未接触小鼠的黑质看起来像夜间的曼哈顿卫星图——数以千计的神经元将多巴胺传送到大脑各处，协调着它们流畅的穿梭、嗅探和啃食。随后，技术人员调出了接触TCE小鼠的大脑扫描。突然之间，我们仿佛置身西弗吉尼亚：不是一片漆黑，但大部分灯都熄灭了，留存的也都调暗了。德·米兰达解释说，多巴胺神经元已经死亡。她还在小鼠身上看到了这些物理后果。“我们观察到轻度的运动缺陷，在它们的步态中都能看出来；我们也看到了认知方面的影响。”

paragraphs.en: De Miranda’s studies, the first ever on inhaled TCE toxicity and Parkinson’s, are compelling, her colleagues agree, and well designed. And although there is more work to be done, the results wrap a bow on Goldman’s epidemiological work and the Parkinson’s Institute’s years of research. TCE is a neurotoxin, and generations of Americans have been exposed. In December 2024, the Environmental Protection Agency finally moved to ban TCE in the United States.
paragraphs.zh: 德·米兰达（De Miranda）的研究是首次针对吸入式三氯乙烯（TCE）毒性与帕金森病关系的研究，同行普遍认为这项研究令人信服，而且设计严谨。虽然仍需进一步工作，这些结果为戈德曼（Goldman）的流行病学研究和帕金森氏病研究所多年的研究画上了一个圆满句号。TCE是一种神经毒素，几代美国人都暴露在它的影响下。直到2024年12月，美国环保署才终于开始在美国禁用TCE。

paragraphs.en: “I think TCE is the most important cause of Parkinson’s in the US,” says Ray Dorsey, the Parkinson’s expert at the University of Rochester. In 2021, Dorsey, who frequently collaborates with De Miranda, Goldman, and a core group of like-minded scientists, published Ending Parkinson’s Disease. The book’s central thesis: Parkinson’s is a growing pandemic, and up to 90 percent of cases are caused by chemicals in our environment. Cut exposures like TCE and pesticides, and we can “end Parkinson’s” as we know it. “The full effect of the Parkinson’s pandemic,” Dorsey writes, “is not inevitable but, to a large extent, preventable.”
paragraphs.zh: 罗切斯特大学的帕金森病专家雷·多尔西（Ray Dorsey）说：“我认为TCE是美国帕金森病最重要的致病因素。”2021年，多尔西与德·米兰达、戈德曼以及一群志趣相投的科学家经常合作，出版了《终结帕金森病》。这本书的核心观点是：帕金森病正在呈上升趋势，像一次流行病一样蔓延，而其中高达90%的病例是由环境中的化学物质引起的。只要减少对TCE和农药等的暴露，我们就能“终结我们所知的帕金森病”。多尔西写道：“帕金森病大流行病的全部影响，并非不可避免，而在很大程度上是可以预防的。”

paragraphs.en: Since the 1990s, the number of Americans with chronic disease has ballooned to more than 75 percent of adults, per the CDC. Autism, insulin resistance, and autoimmune diagnoses have reached epidemic proportions. The incidence of cancer in people under the age of 50 has hit an all-time high. If Parkinson’s disease is—as Ray Dorsey believes—a pandemic that’s being caused by our environment, it’s probably not the only one.
paragraphs.zh: 自1990年代以来，美国人的慢性病人数已膨胀到占成年人的75%以上（数据来自美国疾控中心）。自闭症、胰岛素抵抗和自身免疫疾病的诊断已达到了流行病级别。50岁以下人群中的癌症发病率也创下历史新高。如果帕金森病——正如雷·多尔西所认为——确实是一种由环境导致的流行病，那么它大概并非唯一一例。

paragraphs.en: After a century of putting genetics on a pedestal, the geneticists have some surprising news for us: The vast majority of chronic disease isn’t caused by our genes. “The Human Genome Project was a $3 billion investment, and what did we find out?” says Thomas Hartung, a toxicologist at Johns Hopkins. “Five percent of all disease is purely genetic. Less than 40 percent of diseases even have a genetic component.”
paragraphs.zh: 经历了一个多世纪把遗传学奉为至高无上的崇拜后，遗传学界给我们带来了一则令人惊讶的消息：慢性病中的绝大多数并非由基因引起。约翰·霍普金斯大学的毒理学家托马斯·哈通（Thomas Hartung）表示：“人类基因组计划投入了30亿美元，而我们得出的结论是什么？”他说：“只有5%的所有疾病是纯粹遗传性的。即便从更广义看，少于40%的疾病也具有遗传成分。”

paragraphs.en: Most of the conditions we worry about, instead, stem from a complex interaction between our genes and our environment. Genetics loads the gun, as former National Institutes of Health head Francis Collins put it, but the environment pulls the trigger. Rather than revealing the genetic origins of disease, genomics has done the opposite. Only 10 percent of breast cancer cases are purely genetic. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease? Rheumatoid arthritis? Coronary heart disease? All hover around 20 percent. The primary driver of disease is considerably more terrestrial: It’s the environment, stupid.
paragraphs.zh: 我们担心的大多数疾病，实际上并不是单纯来自基因，而是来自基因与环境之间复杂的互动。正如前任美国国立卫生研究院院长弗朗西斯·柯林斯所说，基因是上膛，环境才扣动扳机。基因组学并没有揭示疾病的遗传起源，反而指向了相反的结论。只有约10%的乳腺癌病例是纯遗传性的。慢性阻塞性肺病？类风湿性关节炎？冠心病？这三者也都大约在20%左右。推动疾病发生的主要因素更有地气一些：就是环境，真的如此。

paragraphs.en: Yet only 1 percent of the roughly 350,000 chemicals in use in the United States have ever been tested for safety. In its 55-year history, the EPA has banned or restricted about a dozen (by contrast, the EU has banned more than 2,000). Paraquat, the pesticide that appears to cause Parkinson’s in farmworkers, has been banned in Europe and China but remains available in the US. And in January, a month after the EPA’s ban on TCE was finalized, the Trump administration moved to undo it, even as new evidence emerged of Parkinson’s clusters in the rust belt, where exposure to trichloroethylene is high.
paragraphs.zh: 然而，在美国在用的约35万种化学物质中，只有约1%曾经接受过安全性测试。在其55年的历史里，美国环保署只禁用或限制了约十几种（相比之下，欧盟已禁用超过2000种）。除草剂百草枯——这种被认为会在农场工人中引发帕金森病的农药——在欧洲和中国都已禁用，但在美国仍然可以买到。并且在1月，当美国环保署对三氯乙烯（TCE）禁令敲定后的一个月，特朗普政府却推动撤销该禁令，尽管此时已有新的证据显示，在“锈带”地区出现了帕金森病聚集，而那里人们接触三氯乙烯的水平很高。

paragraphs.en: It’s easy to mock the MAHAs and the TikTok trad moms making their own food coloring, but the chemical regulatory system in America does not inspire confidence. No one really knows what the chemicals we’re interacting with every day are doing to our bodies.
paragraphs.zh: 我们很容易嘲笑 MAHAs 和那些在 TikTok 上自己做食用色素的“传统妈妈”博主，但美国的化学监管体系并不令人放心。我们并不真正知道，日常接触到的这些化学物质正在对我们的身体做什么。

paragraphs.en: That’s why, earlier this year, slices of brain from Briana De Miranda’s TCE-addled mice ended up with Gary Miller, a professor at Columbia University. Miller is the country’s leading proponent of a brand-new field called exposomics. Your “exposome” is the sum of your own personal environmental exposures, from the womb to the casket. Many exposures, like TCE, disappear from the bloodstream quickly; people who came into contact with a chemical in the past will never be able to prove it. The exposome is a way to potentially answer the question, “Just what the hell have I been exposed to?”
paragraphs.zh: 这也是为什么今年早些时候，布里亚娜·德·米兰达（Briana De Miranda）的TCE处理小鼠脑切片最终交到了哥伦比亚大学教授加里·米勒（Gary Miller）手中。米勒是美国提倡一个全新领域“暴露组学（exposomics）”的领军人物。你的“暴露组（exposome）”是你个人环境暴露的总和，从胎儿期一直延续到生命终点。许多暴露（比如TCE）很快就会从血液中消失；那些过去接触过某种化学物质的人，往往永远无法证明它与自己有关。暴露组学有望回答这样一个问题：我到底接触过什么？

paragraphs.en: Miller began his career in the ’90s as a Parkinson’s researcher studying environmental exposures. But he grew tired of the “whack-a-mole approach” of modern toxicology: identifying one of the 350,000 chemicals on the market as a potential toxicant, looking for the exposure in the environment, looking for correlations, looking for toxicity in mice’s brains, rinse, repeat.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒在90年代开始从事帕金森病研究，研究方向是环境暴露。但他厌倦了现代毒理学中“打地鼠式”的做法：先把市场上35万种化学品中的一种识别为潜在毒物，再寻找其环境暴露，再找相关性，再在小鼠脑中测试毒性，然后循环往复。

paragraphs.en: He wanted a shotgun approach, an answer to the way genome sequencing identifies all the genes in the body. What Miller wants is a Human Exposome Project. “We realized that this wasn’t just about Parkinson’s,” he says. “There were so many disease states we could look at.” Quantify our exposomes, Miller hopes, and we can know what ails us.
paragraphs.zh: 他想要的是一种“猎枪式”方法，一个对应基因测序能够识别人体全部基因的答案。米勒想要的是“人类暴露组计划”。他说："我们意识到这不只是帕金森病的问题，"“有这么多疾病状态可以去研究。”他希望量化我们的暴露组，就能知道是什么在伤害我们的健康。

paragraphs.en: “We have the tools to put the big puzzle together,” says Rima Habre, an environmental health and exposomics expert at the University of Southern California. Through blood draws and metabolomic studies, the exposomics advocates want to measure the vast mixture of chemicals and pollutants in the body and figure out how they impact health. Take air pollution, Habre’s specialty. An ever-changing mélange of small molecules, from tailpipe emissions to tire bits to dust, it has been linked to obesity, endocrine disruption, heart attacks, and more. But if we can figure out what specifically in this toxic cloud is doing the damage, Habre says, we can work to quickly reduce it in our environment, the way we removed lead from gasoline.
paragraphs.zh: 南加州大学的环境健康与暴露组学专家丽玛·哈布尔（Rima Habre）表示，“我们已经有工具把这幅大拼图拼起来。”通过抽血和代谢组学研究，暴露组学倡导者希望测量体内大量混合的化学物质与污染物，并弄清它们如何影响健康。以哈布尔的专长领域——空气污染为例。这种不断变化的混合体由尾气排放、轮胎碎屑到灰尘等小分子构成，已被关联到肥胖、内分泌紊乱、心脏病发作等问题。哈布尔指出，如果我们能找出这团毒性烟雾中究竟是哪部分在造成伤害，就能像当年从汽油中去除铅那样，快速降低其在环境中的暴露。

paragraphs.en: Or autism. Autism diagnoses have exploded from 1 in 10,000 in the ’70s to 1 in 36 today, a rate that genetics and screening can’t explain, says Johns Hopkins’ Thomas Hartung. Hartung, another Human Exposome Project proponent, is growing clusters of neurons in the lab and subjecting them to flame-retardant chemicals—which are applied to couches and car seats across America—to see what happens. Already, the associations trouble him. The goal of all this, Hartung says, is a world where toxicologists like Briana De Miranda don’t have to spend money creating a mouse gas chamber, expose mice for three months, then wait several more months for results.
paragraphs.zh: 再比如自闭症。约翰霍普金斯大学的托马斯·哈顿（Thomas Hartung）说，自20世纪70年代起，自闭症的诊断率已从每1万例中1例上升到如今每36人中就有1例，这一变化无法仅凭遗传和筛查结果来解释。哈顿也是“人类暴露组计划”的倡导者之一，他在实验室中培养神经元簇，并让它们接触阻燃化学品——这些化学品被广泛应用于美国各地的沙发和汽车座椅——以观察其影响。这些相关性已经让他深感不安。哈顿说，所有这些工作的目标，是一个这样的世界：毒理学家如布里安娜·德·米兰达（Briana De Miranda）不必花钱建一个小鼠暴露舱，让小鼠吸入毒气三个月，再等几个月才能拿到结果。

paragraphs.en: Miller’s goal with mice brains is to figure out what exactly about TCE is killing dopamine-producing neurons and leading to Parkinson’s—to unravel and define the interaction between our environment and our genetics in a way never before possible.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒研究小鼠大脑的目标，是弄清楚TCE具体是如何杀死产生多巴胺的神经元并进而导致帕金森病的——以一种前所未有的方式，解开并定义环境与遗传之间的相互作用。

paragraphs.en: The parallels to the Human Genome Project—in both promise and froth—are clear. But there is a sense of empowerment in knowing that our health is not predetermined. Nearly every scientist interviewed for this story does a few simple things. They filter their water, they run an air purifier, they don’t microwave plastic. They don’t freak out about their daily exposures, but they do things like opt for fragrance-free products, avoid eating out of plastic when they can, and buy organic produce. Our exposures, while not always in our control, can be limited.
paragraphs.zh: 这件事与“人类基因组计划”一样，在前景和虚浮感上都十分明显。然而，知道自己的健康并非注定，也是一种力量感。几乎每位接受采访的科学家都会做几件简单的事：过滤水、使用空气净化器，不用微波加热塑料容器里的食物。他们不会对日常接触到的环境因素过度恐慌，但会选择无香精产品，尽量避免用塑料容器盛食物，并购买有机农产品。我们的暴露虽然不总在我们控制之中，却可以被限制。

paragraphs.en: About two hours south of Lejeune in Wilmington, North Carolina, Amy Lindberg is having lunch with her husband, Brad, on a pier overlooking the Atlantic. Although Goldman, De Miranda, and Dorsey have unveiled the likely origins of her Parkinson’s, the random nature of it gnaws at her. “When I was diagnosed, it was just like, where’s everyone else?” Lindberg says. “I felt like, if I have it, what about my coworkers?” She nods to Brad, who also spent years drinking Lejeune’s water. “He suffered no ill consequences,” she says. She worries about her kids, one of whom was born on base.
paragraphs.zh: 在北卡罗来纳州威明顿离勒琼镇南边大约两个小时车程的地方，艾米·林德伯格正与丈夫布拉德在一座俯瞰大西洋的码头吃午饭。虽然戈德曼、德·米兰达和多西在很大程度上揭示了她帕金森病的可能来源，但它的随机性仍让她心有余悸。林德伯格说：“我确诊那会儿，脑子里只有一个念头：其他人呢？”她说，“我当时想，如果我得了这个病，那我的同事们呢？”她朝布拉德点点头——他也曾多年饮用过勒琼的水。她说：“他没有出现任何不良后果。”她担心自己的孩子，其中一个是在军营内出生的。

paragraphs.en: She still exercises constantly, playing pickleball, boxing, and hopping on the elliptical. She’s found that movement, especially high-intensity exercise, reduces her symptoms. A recent Yale study confirmed as much, showing that interval training increases dopaminergic signals in the brains of Parkinson’s patients, suggesting that exercise slows disease progression and even improves neuron function. The environment may have caused Lindberg’s disease, but she can use it to fight back too.
paragraphs.zh: 她现在坚持不断运动，打匹克球、练拳击、坐椭圆机。她发现运动，尤其是高强度运动，能减轻她的症状。最近一项耶鲁大学的研究证实了这一点，显示间歇训练会增强帕金森患者大脑中的多巴胺信号，表明运动可以减缓疾病进展，甚至改善神经元功能。环境可能是林德伯格患病的原因，但她也可以利用环境去反击。
